graph.theorem_statement:
Glassey blow-up argument for the focusing mass-critical nonlinear Schrodinger equation.

Consider a sufficiently regular solution \(u(t,x)\) to the focusing mass-critical NLS on \(\mathbb{R}^d\):

\[
i\partial_t u+\Delta u=-|u|^{4/d}u.
\]

Assume:

- \(u_0\in H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)\),

- \(|x|u_0\in L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)\),

- the energy \(E(u_0)\) is negative, where

\[
E(u)=\frac12\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|\nabla u|^2\,dx
-\frac{1}{2+4/d}\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|u|^{2+4/d}\,dx.
\]

Then the maximal forward existence time \(T\) is finite and

\[
\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty
\quad\text{as }t\uparrow T.
\]

nodes.title: Glassey blow-up for focusing mass-critical NLS
nodes.informal_statement: Under the assumptions \(u_0\in H^1\), \(|x|u_0\in L^2\), and \(E(u_0)<0\), the forward maximal lifespan \(T\) of the focusing mass-critical NLS solution is finite and \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty\) as \(t\uparrow T\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: Define the variance \(V(t)=\int |x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\). The virial computation gives \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\), so \(V\) is strictly concave downward and must reach \(0\) in finite forward time; hence for some finite \(T_*\), \(V(t)\to0\) as \(t\uparrow T_*\). Applying the weighted inequality to \(u(t)\) and using mass conservation yields \(\|u_0\|_2^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_2\), so \(\|\nabla u(t)\|_2\to\infty\) as \(t\uparrow T_*\). Since \(\|u(t)\|_2\) is conserved, also \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}\to\infty\). By the standard \(H^1\) continuation principle, finite-time \(H^1\)-norm blow-up forces the maximal forward lifespan to be finite, and taking \(T\) to be that maximal forward time gives the theorem.

nodes.title: Virial identity with negative constant second derivative
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Differentiate \(V\) once and substitute \(u_t=i\Delta u+i|u|^{4/d}u\). The term \(\int |x|^2|u|^{2+4/d}\,dx\) is real, so after multiplication by \(i\) its real part vanishes. Integrating by parts in the Laplacian term gives \(V'(t)=4\operatorname{Im}\int x\cdot
abla u\,\overline u\,dx\). Differentiate again, substitute formulas for \(u_t\) and \(\overline{u_t}\), and split linear and nonlinear pieces. A standard integration-by-parts computation gives the linear contribution \(8\int |
abla u|^2\,dx\). For the nonlinear contribution, rewrite the real-part expression so that after subtracting the matching term one is left with \(4\int x\cdot 
abla\!\left( rac{d}{2d+4}|u|^{2+4/d}ight)dx\), and integrating by parts using \(\operatorname{div}(x)=d\) yields \(- rac{16}{2+4/d}\int |u|^{2+4/d}\,dx\). Thus \(V''(t)=8\int |
abla u|^2\,dx- rac{16}{2+4/d}\int |u|^{2+4/d}\,dx=16E(u(t))\). Energy conservation then gives \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\).
nodes.formalization_rationale: This is the main self-contained technical step: an explicit virial/energy identity proved by differentiating the variance twice and carrying out nontrivial integration-by-parts and nonlinear coefficient bookkeeping. It discharges substantial inferential burden for the downstream concavity argument, while avoiding broader PDE continuation machinery and avoiding the lower-value elementary concavity consequence.

formal_artifact.lean_statement:
theorem virial_identity_with_negative_constant_second_derivative
    {V E grad nonlin : ℝ → ℝ} {d : ℕ} {E0 : ℝ}
    (hd : 0 < d)
    (hvirial : ∀ t, deriv (deriv V) t =
      8 * grad t - (16 / (2 + 4 / (d : ℝ))) * nonlin t)
    (henergy_def : ∀ t, E t = focusingMassCriticalEnergy d (grad t) (nonlin t))
    (henergy_conserved : ∀ t, E t = E0)
    (hneg : E0 < 0) :
    ∀ t, deriv (deriv V) t = 16 * E t ∧ deriv (deriv V) t = 16 * E0 ∧ deriv (deriv V) t < 0
formal_artifact.lean_code:
import Mathlib

def focusingMassCriticalEnergy (d : ℕ) (grad nonlin : ℝ) : ℝ :=
  (1 / 2 : ℝ) * grad - (1 / (2 + 4 / (d : ℝ))) * nonlin

lemma virial_coefficient_bookkeeping (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (grad nonlin : ℝ) :
    8 * grad - (16 / (2 + 4 / (d : ℝ))) * nonlin =
      16 * focusingMassCriticalEnergy d grad nonlin := by
  unfold focusingMassCriticalEnergy
  have hdR : (d : ℝ) ≠ 0 := by
    exact_mod_cast (Nat.ne_of_gt hd)
  field_simp [hdR]
  ring

theorem virial_identity_with_negative_constant_second_derivative
    {V E grad nonlin : ℝ → ℝ} {d : ℕ} {E0 : ℝ}
    (hd : 0 < d)
    (hvirial : ∀ t, deriv (deriv V) t =
      8 * grad t - (16 / (2 + 4 / (d : ℝ))) * nonlin t)
    (henergy_def : ∀ t, E t = focusingMassCriticalEnergy d (grad t) (nonlin t))
    (henergy_conserved : ∀ t, E t = E0)
    (hneg : E0 < 0) :
    ∀ t, deriv (deriv V) t = 16 * E t ∧ deriv (deriv V) t = 16 * E0 ∧ deriv (deriv V) t < 0 := by
  intro t
  have hcurv_energy : deriv (deriv V) t = 16 * E t := by
    rw [hvirial t, virial_coefficient_bookkeeping d hd (grad t) (nonlin t), ← henergy_def t]
  have hcurv_const : deriv (deriv V) t = 16 * E0 := by
    rw [hcurv_energy, henergy_conserved t]
  refine ⟨hcurv_energy, hcurv_const, ?_⟩
  rw [hcurv_const]
  nlinarith

nodes.title: Weighted \(L^2\) versus gradient inequality
nodes.informal_proof_text: Use \(\operatorname{div}(x)=d\): \(\|f\|_2^2=\frac1d\int (\operatorname{div}x)|f|^2\,dx=-\frac1d\int x\cdot \nabla(|f|^2)\,dx=-\frac{2}{d}\operatorname{Re}\int x\cdot \nabla f\,\overline f\,dx\). Then apply \(|\operatorname{Re} z|\le |z|\) and Cauchy-Schwarz to obtain the stated bound.

nodes.title: Negative virial curvature forces finite-time vanishing of the variance
nodes.informal_statement: If \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\), then \(V(t)\ge0\) cannot hold for all large positive times. Hence there exists a finite \(T_*>0\) such that \(V(T_*)=0\), equivalently \(V(t)\to0\) as \(t\uparrow T_*\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: Because \(V''\) is a negative constant, \(V\) is a concave quadratic-type function with negative leading coefficient. Such a function eventually becomes negative as \(t\to+\infty\). Since by definition \(V(t)=\int |x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\ge0\), this is impossible unless the graph meets \(0\) at some finite forward time. Taking the first such time and using continuity yields \(V(t)\to0\) as \(t\uparrow T_*\).

nodes.title: Variance collapse implies gradient and \(H^1\) blow-up
nodes.informal_statement: Let \(M=\|u_0\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}>0\). Then for all times of existence, \[M^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}.\] Therefore, if \(V(t)\to0\) as \(t\uparrow T_*\), then \(\|\nabla u(t)\|_2\to\infty\), and since \(\|u(t)\|_2=M\) is conserved, also \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}\to\infty\).
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Apply the weighted inequality with \(f=u(t)\). Mass conservation gives \(\|u(t)\|_2=\|u_0\|_2=:M\), and \(M>0\) since \(E(u_0)<0\) rules out the zero solution. Using \(\|xu(t)\|_2^2=V(t)\), one obtains \(M^2\le  rac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|
abla u(t)\|_2\). As \(t\uparrow T_*\), the left side stays fixed while \(\sqrt{V(t)}	o0\), so necessarily \(\|
abla u(t)\|_2	o\infty\). Finally, \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}^2=\|u(t)\|_2^2+\|
abla u(t)\|_2^2\) and \(\|u(t)\|_2=M\) is conserved, hence \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}	o\infty\).

nodes.title: Standard \(H^1\) continuation criterion
nodes.informal_statement: For the mass-critical NLS in \(H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)\), if a solution on \([0,T_*)\) satisfies \(\sup_{t<T_*}\|u(t)\|_{H^1}<\infty\), then it extends beyond \(T_*\). Equivalently, finite-time failure of continuation forces \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}\to\infty\) as \(t\uparrow T_*\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: This is the standard local well-posedness and blow-up alternative in \(H^1\) for the mass-critical nonlinear Schr\"odinger equation. It is used only to identify the finite blow-up time produced by the virial argument with the maximal forward lifespan.

edges.label: uses
edges.explanation: The theorem's norm blow-up conclusion comes from the estimate linking conserved mass, variance, and the gradient.

edges.label: uses
edges.explanation: The maximal-lifespan statement is obtained by combining finite-time \(H^1\)-blow-up with the standard continuation criterion.

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: Finite-time collapse of the variance uses the constant negative second derivative \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\).

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The key lower bound on \(\|\nabla u(t)\|_2\) comes from the weighted \(L^2\)-gradient inequality.

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: To force \(\|\nabla u(t)\|_2\to\infty\), one needs \(V(t)\to0\) at a finite time.
